Strategy Description: Pairs trading on country ETFs where the trader buys and simultaneously sells two ETFs that follow each other when they diverge from their normal synchronized moves.
Core Logic: Country ETFs are often cointegrated due to shared fundamental return correlations. Temporary shocks create statistical arbitrage opportunities when pairs diverge from their historical price band.
Entry Conditions: Divergence between the normalized price series of a pair exceeds 0.5x the historical standard deviation.
Exit Conditions: The pair converges or a 20-day holding period is reached.
Indicators Used: Normalized cumulative total return index, Sum of squared deviations (Distance), Standard Deviation.
Risk Management Rules: Top 5 pairs with the smallest distance are selected; pairs are weighted equally; daily monitoring and rebalancing.
Long/Short Logic: Long the undervalued ETF and short the overvalued ETF.
Market Regime Suitability: Market neutral strategy, though it may show small positive correlation to equity markets.
Volatility Considerations: Estimated annualized daily volatility of approximately 10%.
Full Notes: The strategy uses a 120-day formation period to identify the top 5 pairs with the smallest distance. It is more resilient than stock pairs trading due to the diversified nature of ETFs.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Cointegration, Statistical Arbitrage, Price Normalization, Sum of Squared Deviations.

Strategy Description: A strategy that identifies and rides industry bubbles, exiting when the bubble starts to pop.
Core Logic: Bubbles are defined as structural breaks in an industry's alpha return compared to the market. Excess returns during the bubble phase often offset the risk of the subsequent crash.
Entry Conditions: Industry alpha is statistically significant at a 97.5% threshold based on the CAPM model (or Fama/French 3-factor model) using 10 years of past data.
Exit Conditions: Alpha is no longer statistically significant or monthly rebalancing occurs.
Risk Management Rules: 1/N rule (equal weighting) among all industries experiencing a bubble; no investment if no bubble is detected.
Market Regime Suitability: Bull markets and bubble regimes; not suitable as a hedge for bear markets.
Volatility Considerations: Not explicitly stated, but involves high risk due to bubble dynamics.
Full Notes: Distinct from standard industry momentum; bubbles are identified by abnormal returns that cannot be explained by standard risk factors.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: CAPM, Regression Analysis, Statistical Significance, Structural Breaks.

Strategy Description: A contrarian strategy in the futures market that buys past losers and sells past winners, filtered by trading volume and open interest.
Core Logic: Market overreaction leads to price reversals. High trading volume and low open interest are indicators of irrationality-induced inefficiencies that precede reversals.
Entry Conditions: Contracts in the high-volume, low-open interest group with the lowest (long) or greatest (short) returns in the previous week.
Indicators Used: Weekly Returns, Detrended Trading Volume, Changes in Open Interest.
Risk Management Rules: Contract weights are proportional to the difference between the contract's return and the equal-weighted average return of the group.
Long/Short Logic: Long past losers, short past winners (Contrarian).
Market Regime Suitability: Liquidity providing; often performs well during market crises but carries short-volatility risks.
Full Notes: Uses 24 types of US futures contracts. Volume is detrended by dividing by its sample mean to ensure comparability across markets.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Overreaction Hypothesis, Detrending, Median Filtering, Proportional Weighting.

Strategy Name: Federal Open Market Committee Meeting Effect in Stocks
Strategy Description: A market timing strategy that goes long on stocks only during Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting days.
Core Logic: FOMC meetings are generally positive for the market as the Fed aims to maintain stability and growth, leading to a positive drift in stock prices.
Entry Conditions: Go long (e.g., S&P 500 ETF) at the close one day before the FOMC meeting.
Exit Conditions: Close the position at the end of the meeting day.
Risk Management Rules: Invested in cash during all non-meeting days; low market exposure (approx. 8 days per year).
Market Regime Suitability: Not a hedge for bear markets; exploits specific event-driven optimism.
Volatility Considerations: Low overall portfolio volatility due to high cash allocation.
Full Notes: More than 16% of average yearly returns for equity indices are realized during these few days. The strategy can be easily leveraged.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Calendar Effect, Drift Analysis, Event Study.

Strategy Name: Betting Against Beta Factor in Stocks
Strategy Description: A strategy that goes long on low-beta stocks and short on high-beta stocks, using leverage to neutralize market exposure.
Core Logic: Leverage-constrained investors over-weigh high-beta stocks to achieve higher returns, making them overvalued. Low-beta stocks deliver higher risk-adjusted returns.
Entry Conditions: Rank stocks by beta; long the low-beta decile and short the high-beta decile.
Indicators Used: Beta (calculated using a 1-year rolling window against the market index).
Risk Management Rules: Both portfolios are rescaled to have a beta of one at formation to create a zero-beta portfolio.
Market Regime Suitability: Partial hedge during market turmoil; low-beta stocks are generally safer.
Full Notes: The effect is strongest in small-cap stocks. Caution is needed regarding transaction costs and slippage.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Beta, Leverage Constraints, Risk-Adjusted Returns, Zero-Beta Portfolio Construction.

Strategy Name: Betting Against Beta Factor in International Equities
Category: Equities, Long/Short, Country Picking, Beta Factor
Strategy Description: Applying the 'Betting Against Beta' principle to country equity indexes using ETFs or futures.
Core Logic: Similar to the stock-level BAB, investors' leverage constraints lead to the overvaluation of high-beta countries.
Entry Conditions: Rank country ETFs by beta relative to the MSCI US Equity Index; assign to low-beta and high-beta portfolios.
Risk Management Rules: Portfolios are rescaled to a beta of one; zero-cost, zero-beta construction.
Long/Short Logic: Long low-beta countries, short high-beta countries.
Market Regime Suitability: Partial hedge against equity risk; low-beta countries are safer during turmoil.
Full Notes: The investment universe typically consists of around 13-23 country ETFs. Rebalancing is done every calendar month.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Beta, Leverage Constraints, Portfolio Rescaling, Zero-Beta.

Strategy Description: Exploiting the rise in stock prices around earnings announcement dates, particularly for stocks with high past announcement volume.
Core Logic: Buying pressure from individual investors increases around announcement dates when the news catches their attention, driving prices higher.
Entry Conditions: Rank stocks by volume concentration ratio; long high-volume expected announcers and short high-volume expected non-announcers.
Indicators Used: Volume Concentration Ratio (past 16 announcement months volume / total 48 months volume).
Risk Management Rules: Stocks are value-weighted within portfolios; rebalanced monthly.
Long/Short Logic: Long high-volume announcers, short high-volume non-announcers.
Market Regime Suitability: Unknown; long leg is correlated to the market, short leg may act as a partial hedge.
Full Notes: The premium is strong in large-cap stocks and has been consistent since 1927. It is unrelated to standard market risk factors.

Strategy Description: A calendar anomaly strategy that goes long on the stock market on the final trading day before a holiday.
Core Logic: Behavioral factors such as short-sellers closing positions and general investor optimism around holidays drive positive market moves.
Entry Conditions: Go long on the day preceding a major public holiday (e.g., New Year's, Christmas, Thanksgiving).
Exit Conditions: Close the position at the end of the pre-holiday trading day.
Risk Management Rules: Stay in cash during all other trading days.
Market Regime Suitability: Not suitable as a hedge; timing-based long exposure.
Volatility Considerations: Low overall portfolio volatility due to limited market exposure.
Full Notes: The effect is documented globally. Pre-holiday returns are often ten times larger than average daily returns.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Calendar Anomaly, Behavioral Finance, Short Covering.

Strategy Description: A strategy that selects mutual funds based on their past performance, exploiting the momentum effect in fund returns.
Core Logic: Investors tend to chase performance, and there is a strong momentum effect between equity styles and management strategies.
Entry Conditions: Sort no-load equity funds by past 6-month return; select the top decile.
Risk Management Rules: Equal weighting of selected funds; universe limited to no-load funds to avoid fees.
Market Regime Suitability: Not suitable as a hedge; long-only momentum exposure.
Full Notes: Other measures like closeness to 1-year high in NAV and momentum factor loading also provide independent predictive power.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Performance Chasing, Style Momentum, Decile Sorting.

Strategy Description: A strategy that buys stocks close to their 52-week highs and shorts those far from them, focusing on industry-level information.
Core Logic: Investors use the 52-week high as an anchor and under-react to positive news as prices approach this level, leading to subsequent continuation.
Entry Conditions: Calculate the ratio of current price to 52-week high; long stocks in the 6 industries with the highest weighted average ratios and short the 6 lowest.
Indicators Used: Price / 52-Week High Ratio, Industry Market Capitalization.
Risk Management Rules: Equal weighting; monthly rebalancing of 1/3 of the portfolio; avoid implementation in January.
Market Regime Suitability: Unknown; independent of standard momentum effect.
Full Notes: The effect is mainly driven by under-reaction to industry information rather than firm-specific news.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Anchoring Bias, Under-reaction, Weighted Averages, Industry Momentum.
